items.title: Sync your feature branch with the latest main
items.scenario: Your feature branch is three days old; `main` has moved on with five commits. You want your branch to include them.
items.explanation: `git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main` (on your feature branch). Your three feature commits replay on top of the new `main`. Resolve any conflicts once per commit, push with `--force-with-lease`.

items.title: Squash 12 'wip' commits into one before opening the PR
items.scenario: While building, you committed often (good!) but ended with 12 noisy commits the reviewer doesn't need to see.
items.explanation: `git rebase -i main` → mark commits 2-12 as `squash` (or `fixup`) → write one clean message. Reviewer sees a single coherent commit.

items.title: Land a feature with a visible merge commit
items.scenario: Team policy: every feature lands as a merge commit so the branch boundary is visible in history.
items.explanation: `git merge --no-ff feature/auth` — even though fast-forward is possible, `--no-ff` forces a merge commit you can later revert with `git revert -m 1 <merge_sha>`.

items.title: Bisect to find the eval regression
items.scenario: An eval score dropped 4 points between yesterday and today; 18 commits landed in between.
items.explanation: `git bisect start; git bisect bad HEAD; git bisect good HEAD~18` then run the eval on each commit Git checks out — finds the bad commit in log₂(18) ≈ 5 steps. Linear history (rebased PRs) makes this dead simple.
